schools.summary: Paul illustrates from the law of inheritance: an 'heir,' 'as long as he is a child' (a minor, nepios, in his nonage), 'differeth nothing from a servant' — though destined to own all, in his minority he is under restraint like a slave; 'though he be lord of all' (the rightful owner of the whole estate), he has not yet entered on his inheritance (Barnes, Robertson).

Luther (with Calvin, Tertullian): this pictures the church under the old covenant — heirs of the promise, yet held in the bondage of the law as a tutor and schoolmaster until the time of fulfilment, nourished in hope of the liberty to come.

themes: the heir, as long as he is a child, differeth nothing from a servant
though he be lord of all
the heir in minority no different from a servant
the church under the law

schools.summary: The minor heir 'is under tutors (epitropous — guardians of his person) and governors (oikonomous — stewards of his estate)' — placed under the control and care of others during his nonage, not free to act for himself; and this subjection lasts only 'until the time appointed of the father' (prothesmia — a fixed term, set by the father, at which the heir comes of age and enters his freedom and inheritance) — Barnes, Robertson.
Luther: Moses, as tutor and governor, held the church under the law's discipline until the appointed time — the coming of Christ, who ends the time of law and begins the time of grace.

themes: under tutors and governors until the time appointed of the father
the heir under tutors until the appointed time
the law's temporary discipline
the father's fixed term

schools.summary: Paul applies the figure: 'we' (chiefly the Jews, but including all before Christ), 'when we were children' (in religious nonage), 'were in bondage' under the old dispensation; 'the elements (stoicheia, rudiments) of the world' meaning the elementary, rudimentary teaching of religion — the ceremonial law, the ABCs or first principles, called 'of the world' because outward, material, mundane, transient and of little value, including no clear recognition of spiritual things (the same phrase as Colossians 2:8, 20; Barnes, Ellicott, Clarke).

Calvin: the 'elements' are rudiments — under the old covenant we did not enjoy the truth in a simple form, but involved in earthly figures; what was outward was 'of the world,' though a heavenly mystery was concealed under it.

Some older commentators (noted by Ellicott and Robertson) took 'the elements of the world' in the sense of the physical elements of nature or the heavenly bodies — either as objects of Gentile worship or as marking the times of the Jewish festivals; but the rudimentary-teaching sense is generally preferred.

debates.question: What are 'the elements (stoicheia) of the world'?

sides.position: The rudiments / first principles of religion — the ceremonial law, outward and elementary
The physical elements of nature or the heavenly bodies (older view, noted but not preferred)

themes: when we were children, were in bondage under the elements of the world
the church in its nonage in bondage
the elements of the world
rudiments or physical elements

schools.summary: 'The fulness of the time' was the complete, predetermined season ordained by God — when prophecy converged, the world lay at peace under Rome, the Greek tongue was widespread, and all was ripe for the Redeemer; 'God sent forth his Son' implies the Son's pre-existence and deity (he was with God before he was sent, John 1:1; Philippians 2:6); 'made under the law,' subject to its precept and curse, to redeem those under it. On 'made (born) of a woman,' Ellicott and Robertson hold it a common idiom (Matthew 11:11) denoting simply Christ's true humanity, with no direct reference to the miraculous conception (though his deity is affirmed by 'his Son').

Calvin (with Gill, Clarke, Wesley): 'made of a woman' denotes the virgin birth — Christ formed of the substance of his mother, not by ordinary generation; he, the eternal Son, having two natures, became subject to the law in our room, that exemption from it might be obtained for us; the redemption is from the law with all its appendages, not from the obedience owed to its moral rule.

Cyril: against the heretics who make Christ's birth a phantom, or the child of Joseph and Mary — Mary was a virgin betrothed, called Joseph's 'wife' only by betrothal; the Son was truly born of the Virgin.

debates.question: Does 'made of a woman' refer to the virgin birth or simply to Christ's true humanity?

sides.position: The virgin birth / miraculous conception — formed of his mother's substance, not by ordinary generation
A common idiom for true humanity; no direct reference to the miraculous conception (though it agrees with it)

themes: when the fulness of the time was come, God sent forth his Son
made of a woman, made under the law
the incarnation in the fulness of time
the pre-existent Son made man

schools.summary: The first purpose of the incarnation: 'to redeem (exagorazo — buy back, ransom) them that were under the law' — to free those held in bondage under the law's precept and curse; the second, 'that we might receive the adoption of sons (huiothesia)' — being redeemed from slavery, believers are taken into God's family as sons, with the full rights of sonship (Barnes, Robertson).

Luther (with Calvin): Christ entered the holy place by his own blood and obtained eternal redemption, so that those once slaves under the law become free sons and heirs; redemption issues in adoption.

themes: To redeem them that were under the law, that we might receive the adoption of sons
the twofold purpose: redemption and adoption
freed from the law, taken into God's family
redemption issuing in sonship

schools.summary: 'Because ye are sons' (already adopted), God sends 'the Spirit of his Son' — the Holy Spirit, the Spirit of Christ — 'into your hearts'; the Spirit is the seal and evidence of sonship, called 'the Spirit of his Son' because he proceeds from and belongs to the Son as to the Father (Romans 8:9); and the Spirit makes the believer cry 'Abba, Father' — the Aramaic and Greek words together expressing the intimate, confident address of a child to his father, both Jew and Gentile crying to God as Father (Barnes, Robertson).

Cyril (with Calvin, Luther): the Spirit is the personal, living Spirit who is God, an incidental witness to the Trinity; he it is who works in the heart the filial cry, assuring the believer of his adoption.

themes: God hath sent forth the Spirit of his Son into your hearts, crying, Abba, Father
the Spirit of the Son sent into the heart
the filial cry Abba, Father
the Spirit the seal of adoption

schools.summary: Paul draws the conclusion ('wherefore'): 'thou art no more a servant (slave, in bondage under the law) but a son' — the believer's status is changed from slave to son; and 'if a son, then an heir' — sonship carries heirship — 'an heir of God' (heir to the inheritance God gives) 'through Christ' (by virtue of union with Christ, the great Heir; Barnes, Robertson).
Luther (with Calvin): the believer, once a slave under the law, is now a free son and heir of all God's blessings; the whole argument of the epistle — freedom in Christ — is sealed here.

themes: thou art no more a servant, but a son; and if a son, then an heir of God through Christ
the believer no longer a servant but a son and heir
sonship carrying heirship
liberty in Christ

schools.summary: Paul recalls the Galatians' former state: 'when ye knew not God' (in their heathen ignorance), 'ye did service (edouleusate — were in bondage, served as slaves) unto them which by nature are no gods' — the idols they once worshipped, which had no real divinity, being only the work of men's hands or the creatures of imagination; the Galatians had served what was nothing (Barnes, Robertson).
Henry: Paul reminds them of their former slavery to false gods in order to expose the folly of returning, now that they know the true God, to any form of bondage.

themes: when ye knew not God, ye did service unto them which by nature are no gods
the Galatians' former bondage to false gods
idols that are no gods
slavery to nothing

schools.summary: Paul says they now 'know God,' but corrects himself — 'or rather are known of God' — for their knowing him springs from his first knowing (choosing, owning) them; grace is the source. The astonished rebuke: 'how turn ye again to the weak and beggarly elements (the same stoicheia, the rudiments)' — the ceremonial observances, called 'weak' (powerless to justify or save) and 'beggarly' (poor, bringing no spiritual riches) — 'whereunto ye desire again to be in bondage' (Barnes, Robertson).
Luther (with Calvin): to take up the law's ceremonies as necessary to righteousness is to return to slavery — exchanging the liberty of Christ for the very yoke they had escaped.

themes: after that ye have known God, or rather are known of God
how turn ye again to the weak and beggarly elements
the rebuke for turning back
weak and beggarly elements

schools.summary: Paul instances their Judaizing: 'ye observe (paratereisthe — scrupulously watch) days (sabbaths and fast-days), and months (new moons), and times (the seasonal feasts — Passover, Pentecost, Tabernacles), and years (the sabbatical year and jubilee)' — taking on the whole Jewish ceremonial calendar, an exhaustive list of Mosaic observances. Barnes extends the warning beyond the Jewish feasts to all days not commanded in Scripture — including saints' days and even Protestant festivals — as equally inconsistent with gospel freedom; Ellicott (citing Origen's spiritual reading) holds Christian seasons are not necessarily condemned, but belong to the 'elements,' and it is safest to follow church order intelligently.

Calvin (with Wesley, Robertson, Luther): no condemnation is given to the civil reckoning of days (the order of nature, Genesis 8:22), nor to free and orderly Christian observance void of superstition; what Paul reproves is the observance that binds the conscience by religious considerations — treating days as holy in themselves, one day more holy than another, calendar-keeping as necessary to the worship of God and to righteousness (the Judaizers' demand). Robertson notes Paul kept these feasts as a Jew but objected to Gentiles taking them as a means of salvation; Luther: the false apostles taught these as necessary to righteousness, and consciences are not to be so bound.

debates.question: Does this verse condemn all observance of holy days, or only Judaizing/meritorious observance?

sides.position: Only the observance that binds conscience and is made necessary to righteousness; not civil order or free Christian observance
Extends to all days not commanded in Scripture — saints' days and even Protestant festivals are as inconsistent with gospel freedom
Christian seasons are not necessarily condemned, but belong to the 'elements'; follow church order intelligently (citing Origen)

themes: Ye observe days, and months, and times, and years
the Galatians' observance of the Jewish calendar
which observance is condemned
righteousness by calendar a bondage

schools.summary: Paul's tender fear: 'I am afraid of (for) you, lest I have bestowed upon you labour in vain' — he dreads that all his toil in planting the gospel among them may prove fruitless if they fall back into bondage; if they abandon the gospel of grace for the law, his ministry among them would have come to nothing, their relapse frustrating his labour (Barnes, Robertson).
Luther (with Calvin): the danger is real, for to seek righteousness by the law is to fall from grace; Paul's fear shows how serious their drift was.

themes: I am afraid of you, lest I have bestowed upon you labour in vain
Paul's fear that his labour was in vain
dread of their relapse
pastoral solicitude

schools.summary: Paul turns from argument to tender appeal: 'brethren, I beseech you, be as I am' — be free from the law, as I am — 'for I am as ye are' (I became as a Gentile, free from the law, for your sake, 1 Corinthians 9:21); he asks them to follow his example of liberty, and adds 'ye have not injured me at all' — he bears them no personal grievance, his appeal springing not from any wrong done to him but from love and concern for them (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).
Luther (with Calvin): after sharp rebuke, Paul softens, calling them 'brethren' and pleading with affection; the pastor mixes severity with love.

themes: Brethren, I beseech you, be as I am; for I am as ye are
ye have not injured me at all
Paul's affectionate appeal to be as he is
pleading from love

schools.summary: Paul recalls that 'through infirmity (weakness, sickness) of the flesh I preached the gospel unto you at the first' — it was some bodily ailment that was the occasion of, or attended, his first preaching among the Galatians; he labored among them under a real physical weakness or illness (perhaps the same 'thorn in the flesh,' 2 Corinthians 12:7), his outward condition unimpressive, even repellent (Barnes, Robertson).
Luther (with Calvin): despite Paul's weak and afflicted appearance, the Galatians had received his gospel gladly; their first love stands in sharp contrast to their present coldness.

themes: through infirmity of the flesh I preached the gospel unto you at the first
Paul first preaching amid bodily infirmity
the thorn in the flesh
their warm first reception

schools.summary: Paul's 'temptation (trial) which was in my flesh' (his bodily affliction, a trial to him and a test to them) they 'despised not, nor rejected (loathed, spurned)' — his infirmity did not make them turn from him; rather they received Paul 'as an angel (messenger) of God' — even 'as Christ Jesus' himself — with the highest reverence and love, as though Christ spoke in him (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley (with Calvin): such was their first devotion that they honoured the afflicted apostle as if he were Christ — which makes their present readiness to forsake him the more grievous.

themes: my temptation which was in my flesh ye despised not, nor rejected
received me as an angel of God, even as Christ Jesus
receiving Paul as an angel of God
their warm first devotion

schools.summary: Paul asks 'where is then the blessedness (the happiness, the self-felicitation) ye spake of?' — the joy and congratulation they once felt at having received the gospel; where had it gone? And the proverbial expression of their love: 'if it had been possible, ye would have plucked out your own eyes (your most precious members) and given them to me' — they would have parted with anything, even their eyes, for Paul's sake (Barnes, Robertson; some find here a hint of Paul's eye-affliction).
Luther (with Calvin): Paul recalls their once-boundless love to expose, by contrast, the coldness into which the false teachers had drawn them.

themes: Where is then the blessedness ye spake of
ye would have plucked out your own eyes, and have given them to me
their former blessedness and self-giving love
the lost first love

schools.summary: Paul asks, with grief, 'am I therefore become your enemy, because I tell you the truth?' — has his faithful telling of the truth (in this very letter, and before) turned their love into hostility? The painful irony being that those who once loved him as Christ now regard him as an enemy, and for no other cause than that he speaks the truth to them; faithfulness has been requited with coldness (Barnes, Robertson).
Luther (with Calvin): it is the perpetual experience of faithful ministers that telling unwelcome truth makes them seem enemies; flatterers are loved, truth-tellers resented.

themes: Am I therefore become your enemy, because I tell you the truth
Paul asking if truth made him their enemy
love turned to hostility
the lot of faithful ministers

schools.summary: Paul exposes the Judaizers: 'they zealously affect you (court you, make much of you, pay you eager attention) but not well (not honourably, with no good motive)' — their zeal is a flattering courtship with a selfish end; their real aim being 'they would exclude you (shut you out — from Paul, from gospel liberty, from the wider church)' so 'that ye might affect (court, be devoted to) them' — to make the Galatians dependent on themselves alone (Barnes, Robertson).
Henry (with Calvin): the false teachers sought to monopolize the Galatians' devotion for their own glory, whereas Paul sought their good; selfish ambition is the mark of the false.

themes: They zealously affect you, but not well
they would exclude you, that ye might affect them
the false teachers' self-serving zeal
selfish ambition masked as zeal

schools.summary: Paul grants that zeal itself is not bad: 'it is good to be zealously affected (to be the object of earnest attention, or to be earnest) always in a good thing' — zeal is commendable when its object is good; and his wish is that their zeal for the good be constant, 'not only when I am present with you' but always, steady and not dependent on his presence — unlike the false teachers' selfish and fitful courtship (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: true zeal is directed to a good end and endures whether the apostle is present or absent; Paul would have their devotion to the truth steady and constant.

themes: it is good to be zealously affected always in a good thing
not only when I am present with you
zeal good when in a good cause and constant
the mark of right zeal

schools.summary: Paul's deeply affectionate address: 'my little children (teknia)' — a term of the tenderest love, as a parent to little ones, he being their spiritual father; the striking figure that he is 'in travail (the pangs of childbirth) again' for them, as if their relapse had undone his earlier labour, so that he must endure the pains of bringing them to spiritual birth a second time; 'until Christ be formed in you' — until Christ's image, his life and likeness, is fully shaped within them, the aim of all his travail (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).

Luther (with Calvin): the goal is that Christ be formed in them — their full conformity to Christ; Paul labours not for outward conformity to the law but for the inward shaping of Christ's life in the soul.

themes: My little children, of whom I travail in birth again
until Christ be formed in you
Paul travailing in birth again
Christ formed in them the goal

schools.summary: Paul's wish: 'I desire to be present with you now, and to change my voice' — he longs to be with them in person, so as to vary his tone (to speak more tenderly, or to adapt his words to their state, as a letter cannot); for he 'stands in doubt (aporoumai — is perplexed, at a loss) of (about) you' — uncertain how to deal with them, what to think of their state, torn between hope and fear, not knowing whether to be severe or gentle (Barnes, Robertson).

Luther (with Calvin): a faithful minister, perplexed by a wavering people, longs to be present that he may read their hearts and suit his words; the cold medium of a letter cannot do what a living voice can.

themes: I desire to be present with you now, and to change my voice
for I stand in doubt of you
Paul's wish to be present and change his tone
perplexed over their state

schools.summary: Paul turns to argue from Scripture: 'tell me, ye that desire to be under the law' (who wish to be subject to the Mosaic law) 'do ye not hear (heed, understand) the law?' — do they not listen to what the law itself teaches? 'The law' here means the Pentateuch, the books of Moses — the very Scriptures the legalists revere; Paul will draw from them (the history of Abraham's sons) an argument against the legalists' own position (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).
Henry: Paul meets the Judaizers on their own ground, appealing to the law they prize to show that it testifies for grace and freedom, not bondage.

themes: ye that desire to be under the law, do ye not hear the law
Paul challenging the legalists from the law itself
the law meaning the Pentateuch
meeting them on their own ground

schools.summary: Paul cites the record (Genesis): 'Abraham had two sons' — Ishmael, 'by a bondmaid (Hagar, the slave),' and Isaac, 'by a freewoman (Sarah, the free wife)' — the historical fact on which the allegory will rest; the foundational distinction being that one son was born of a slave, the other of the free wife, a difference of mothers that Paul will use to picture the difference between the two covenants (Barnes, Robertson).
Luther (with Calvin): Abraham, with his two sons by two women, pictures God with two kinds of people — the children of bondage and the children of freedom; the history is the seed of the allegory.

themes: Abraham had two sons, the one by a bondmaid, the other by a freewoman
the two sons of Abraham
the bondmaid and the freewoman
the historical ground of the allegory

schools.summary: The key difference: Ishmael, 'of the bondwoman, was born after the flesh' — by ordinary, natural generation, the result of Abraham and Hagar's own contrivance; while Isaac, 'of the freewoman, was by promise' — born through God's special promise, beyond nature (Sarah being past age); the contrast 'after the flesh' (the way of nature and human effort) against 'by promise' (the way of grace and divine power), Ishmael representing what man produces, Isaac what God gives (Barnes, Robertson).
Luther (with Calvin): Isaac, the child of promise, pictures believers born not by the works of the law (the flesh) but by the promise of grace; the manner of birth is the heart of the allegory.

themes: he who was of the bondwoman was born after the flesh; but he of the freewoman was by promise
the son after the flesh and the son of promise
nature and human effort vs grace and promise
the heart of the allegory

schools.summary: Paul does not deny the historical truth of the narrative, nor make it mere fable: he uses the real history of Abraham's sons as a type or illustration of a spiritual truth (as 'types or figures,' 1 Corinthians 10:11), putting a secondary, deeper sense on the narrative — distinct from the fanciful, mystery-finding allegorizing of Origen and the Alexandrian school, which Barnes, Clarke, and Ellicott reject as a corruption that made the Bible a book of enigmas. Hagar and Sarah 'are (represent, stand for) the two covenants' — the one 'from mount Sinai, which gendereth to bondage' (the legal covenant, bearing children unto slavery), 'which is Agar' (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

Calvin: as the house of Abraham was then the true Church, the chief events in it are so many types for us; the argumentative force rests on the apostolic assertion of Christian liberty, not on the logical cogency of the inference from type to thing typified. Doctrine is the mother of whom we are born — the legal covenant (Hagar/Sinai) gendering to bondage, the evangelical to freedom.

debates.question: Is Paul's 'allegory' the same as Origenist allegorizing, or the typological use of real history?

sides.position: The typological/illustrative use of a true historical narrative — a secondary spiritual sense applied to real events
The fanciful, mystery-finding allegorizing of Origen and the Alexandrians — explicitly rejected as a corruption of Scripture
holders: Origen's school (rejected by Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott)

themes: Which things are an allegory: for these are the two covenants
the one from the mount Sinai, which gendereth to bondage, which is Agar
the allegory of the two covenants
allegory versus type

schools.summary: Paul's correspondence: 'this Agar (Hagar) is mount Sinai in Arabia' — Hagar represents the Sinai covenant (Luther and others note 'Hagar' was even an Arabian name for the mountain, perhaps suggesting the connection) — 'and answereth to (sustoichei — corresponds with, stands in the same rank/column as) Jerusalem which now is'; the 'present Jerusalem' (the centre of Judaism, the law, and the temple worship) 'is in bondage with her children' — she and her people (the unbelieving Jews and the Judaizers) are slaves under the law, like Hagar and her son (Barnes, Robertson).
Luther (with Calvin): the legal Jerusalem, for all her boasted privilege, is the slave-mother bearing slave-children — those who seek righteousness by the law remain in bondage.

themes: this Agar is mount Sinai in Arabia, and answereth to Jerusalem which now is
is in bondage with her children
Hagar and Sinai answering to the present Jerusalem
legal Jerusalem in bondage

schools.summary: The antithesis to the earthly Jerusalem: 'Jerusalem which is above (the heavenly Jerusalem, the spiritual city of God, the church and commonwealth of the redeemed) is free' — not in bondage to the law, but the home of gospel liberty; and she 'is the mother of us all' — of all believers, Jew and Gentile; corresponding to Sarah the freewoman, she bears free children by the gospel of promise (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril (with Luther, Calvin): the heavenly Jerusalem, the church of the new covenant, is the true mother of the faithful, who are born free by grace, not slaves under the law.

themes: Jerusalem which is above is free, which is the mother of us all
the heavenly Jerusalem, free
the mother of believers
born free by grace

schools.summary: Paul quotes Isaiah 54:1: 'Rejoice, thou barren that bearest not... for the desolate hath many more children than she which hath an husband' — a prophecy of the abundant offspring of the once-barren; applied to Sarah and the heavenly Jerusalem, the 'barren' and 'desolate' (Sarah, long childless; the church of the Gentiles, once without the promises) is to rejoice, for she shall have 'many more children' than the married woman (the synagogue, the legal Jerusalem) — the free mother's offspring exceeding the bond (Barnes, Robertson).
Luther (with Calvin): the church of grace, once seemingly barren and forsaken, becomes fruitful beyond the legal synagogue, filling the world with free children of promise.

themes: Rejoice, thou barren that bearest not
the desolate hath many more children than she which hath an husband
the prophecy of the barren woman's many children
the fruitfulness of the free mother

schools.summary: Paul applies the allegory directly: 'now we, brethren, as Isaac was, are the children of promise' — believers correspond to Isaac, born not by the flesh but by God's promise; they are the free, promised offspring, born by the power of God's promise and grace (like Isaac, born beyond nature), not by works of the law — their sonship rests on grace (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).
Wesley: as Isaac, the son of promise, was the true heir, so believers, the children of promise, are the true heirs of the inheritance — free, not slaves.

themes: we, brethren, as Isaac was, are the children of promise
believers as children of promise like Isaac
born by grace, not works
the true heirs

schools.summary: Paul observes that 'then' (in Abraham's house) 'he that was born after the flesh (Ishmael) persecuted (mocked, harassed, Genesis 21:9) him that was born after the Spirit (Isaac)' — the elder, fleshly son persecuting the younger, promised son; 'even so it is now' — the children of the flesh (the unbelieving Jews and Judaizers, who trust the law) persecute the children of the Spirit (the believers, born of promise); the pattern repeats (Barnes, Robertson).
Luther (with Calvin): it has ever been the lot of the children of promise to be persecuted by the children of the law; the believer should not be dismayed but expect such opposition.

themes: he that was born after the flesh persecuted him that was born after the Spirit, even so it is now
the child of the flesh persecuting the child of the Spirit
the recurring pattern of opposition
the lot of the children of promise

schools.summary: Paul appeals to Scripture's decisive word (Genesis 21:10, Sarah's demand, ratified by God): 'cast out (expel, send away) the bondwoman (Hagar) and her son (Ishmael)' — the slave and her child are to be put out of the house; the ground being that the slave's son 'shall not be heir with' the free son — the two cannot share the inheritance, the child of bondage excluded from the inheritance reserved for the child of promise (Barnes, Robertson).

Luther (with Calvin): the covenant of law and its children (the Judaizers, those who seek righteousness by works) must be cast out; they cannot inherit with the children of grace — law and gospel cannot be mingled as the ground of righteousness.

themes: Cast out the bondwoman and her son
the son of the bondwoman shall not be heir with the son of the freewoman
the Scripture's verdict to cast out the bondwoman
the disinheriting of the law's children

schools.summary: Paul draws the conclusion ('so then') of the whole allegory: 'we are not children of the bondwoman (Hagar, the law, bondage) but of the free (Sarah, the gospel, liberty)' — believers belong to the free mother; the triumphant result being that believers are free children, heirs of the promise and of gospel freedom, not slaves under the law; their standing is that of Isaac, the free son and heir (Barnes, Robertson).
Luther (with Calvin): this is the heart of the epistle — the believer is free from the law as a means of righteousness, a child of the free covenant of grace, and must not return to bondage.

themes: we are not children of the bondwoman, but of the free
believers are children of the free
gospel freedom, not bondage
heirs of the promise like Isaac
